groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Episode subject, objective, audience, core content, format, language, target duration, host identity, podcast identity, listener action, and source material.

groups.title: Episode Structure
groups.description: Script depth, opening, segment count, required sections, examples, transitions, listener interaction, and recurring show elements.

groups.title: Voice & Delivery
groups.description: Host voice, pacing, energy, language complexity, humor, pronunciation guidance, and words or phrases to avoid.

groups.title: Guests, Sponsors & Conversion
groups.description: Guest participation, interview boundaries, sponsor messages, sponsor placement, and CTA details.

groups.title: Publishing, Discovery & Safety
groups.description: Publishing context, series continuity, sensitive-content handling, show notes, discovery keywords, links, and additional requirements.

variables.label: Episode topic
variables.placeholder: Example: Why small creators struggle to stay consistent
variables.hint: State the main subject or question the episode should explore.

help.what: Provide the episode topic for this podcast episode.
help.why: The model cannot determine the episode’s central subject without user input.
help.example: Good example: Why small creators struggle to stay consistent
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Primary goal
variables.hint: Choose the most important result the episode should achieve.

help.what: Provide the primary goal for this podcast episode.
help.why: The same topic requires a different structure depending on the intended outcome.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Target listeners
variables.placeholder: Who are they, what do they know, and why would they listen?
variables.hint: Describe the intended listeners, their knowledge level, interests, or challenges.

help.what: Provide the target listeners for this podcast episode.
help.why: Audience knowledge and motivation materially affect language, examples, depth, and pacing.
help.example: Good example: Solo creators who understand content strategy but struggle to keep a weekly publishing rhythm.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Core message
variables.placeholder: What should listeners understand, feel, or remember afterward?
variables.hint: Summarize the episode’s main takeaway in one or two clear ideas.

help.what: Provide the core message for this podcast episode.
help.why: A defined takeaway prevents the episode from becoming unfocused.
help.example: Good example: A repeatable content workflow beats rebuilding every prompt from scratch.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Key points and facts
variables.placeholder: List the arguments, facts, examples, steps, or questions that must appear.
variables.hint: Include the essential material the script must cover.

help.what: Provide the key points and facts for this podcast episode.
help.why: These details determine the factual substance and progression of the episode.
help.example: Good example: Templates reduce decision fatigue, saved prompts preserve what works, and consistency improves when the workflow is simple.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Episode format
variables.hint: Choose the format that best matches how the episode will be recorded.

help.what: Provide the episode format for this podcast episode.
help.why: Each podcast format requires a different script architecture and speaker treatment.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Output language
variables.hint: Specify the language in which the complete output should be written.

help.what: Provide the output language for this podcast episode.
help.why: The recording script and supporting material must use the user’s publishing language.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Target duration
variables.hint: The script will use an approximate spoken-word target rather than an exact runtime guarantee.

help.what: Provide the target duration for this podcast episode.
help.why: Duration determines script depth, segment timing, question count, and approximate word count.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported claims, broad topics without a clear angle, or changing the requested episode into a different subject.

variables.label: Host name
variables.hint: Used in the introduction and closing when appropriate.

help.what: Optional context for the host name field.
help.why: A supplied host name makes the spoken introduction usable without manual replacement.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Podcast name
variables.hint: Leave blank when the show name should not be mentioned.

help.what: Optional context for the podcast name field.
help.why: The show name may be required for a recording-ready introduction and outro.
help.example: Good example: The Independent Creator Show
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Listener action
variables.hint: Choose one primary action or let the model select a low-pressure conclusion.

help.what: Optional context for the listener action field.
help.why: The episode closing should support the user’s actual goal without adding competing calls to action.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Sources and reference material
variables.placeholder: Paste verified facts, quotes, research notes, links, or source excerpts.
variables.hint: Only supplied or clearly established information may be presented as factual.

help.what: Optional context for the sources and reference material field.
help.why: Source material improves factual precision and prevents unsupported research claims.
help.example: Good example: Use only the supplied workflow notes; do not invent creator counts, revenue, or research.
help.avoid: Avoid invented facts, links, credentials, quotations, sponsor claims, guest details, or private information that was not supplied.

variables.label: Script detail
variables.hint: Select a complete script, flexible talking points, or a combination of both.

help.what: Optional context for the script detail field.
help.why: Different hosts need different levels of scripting and improvisational freedom.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Opening style
variables.hint: Controls the opening moments before the main host introduction.

help.what: Optional context for the opening style field.
help.why: The opening style materially changes the first minute and listener-retention approach.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Number of segments
variables.hint: Use 2–8 for most episodes or leave the default.

help.what: Optional context for the number of segments field.
help.why: This gives the user direct control over the episode’s structural complexity.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Sections to include
variables.placeholder: Select any required sections; or list multiple choices separated by commas.
variables.hint: Choose only sections that are important to this episode. Available options: Opening hook, Host introduction, Topic context, Main discussion, Practical example, Listener question, Key takeaways, Call to action, Closing outro.

help.what: Optional context for the sections to include field.
help.why: Some shows have required structural elements that cannot be inferred reliably.
help.example: Good example: Opening hook, main discussion, key takeaways, closing outro
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Stories or examples
variables.placeholder: Describe any real story, case, analogy, demonstration, or example to include.
variables.hint: Do not include private details or stories that are not approved for publication.

help.what: Optional context for the stories or examples field.
help.why: User-supplied examples make the episode specific without requiring fabricated anecdotes.
help.example: Good example: Use the approved example of a creator saving three reusable prompt formats.
help.avoid: Avoid fake personal stories, customer stories, case studies, testimonials, or dialogue presented as real.

variables.label: Transition style
variables.hint: Controls how the script moves between topics and segments.

help.what: Optional context for the transition style field.
help.why: Transition style affects how formal, conversational, or tightly produced the episode feels.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Listener interaction
variables.placeholder: Select interaction elements; or list multiple choices separated by commas.
variables.hint: Add only interaction methods that fit the episode naturally. Available options: Auto, Reflection question, Listener challenge, Comment prompt, Q&A invitation, Poll suggestion, Listener submission request, No interaction.

help.what: Optional context for the listener interaction field.
help.why: The user may want participation beyond a standard promotional CTA.
help.example: Good example: Reflection question, comment prompt
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Recurring show elements
variables.placeholder: Add recurring segment names, catchphrases, rituals, or intro conventions.
variables.hint: Include only established elements the show already uses.

help.what: Optional context for the recurring show elements field.
help.why: Existing show conventions must be supplied rather than invented.
help.example: Good example: End each episode with one practical workflow question.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Tone
variables.hint: Choose the dominant tone; the model will avoid exaggerated delivery.

help.what: Optional context for the tone field.
help.why: Tone affects vocabulary, sentence rhythm, framing, and emotional intensity.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Host voice or style
variables.placeholder: Describe how the host normally speaks or paste a short approved sample.
variables.hint: Mention sentence style, personality, preferred expressions, or level of formality.

help.what: Optional context for the host voice or style field.
help.why: A supplied voice description helps the script sound like the actual host.
help.example: Good example: Practical, warm, slightly wry; short sentences around important points.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Pacing
variables.hint: Controls information density and the amount of space between ideas.

help.what: Optional context for the pacing field.
help.why: Pacing determines sentence length, pauses, segment density, and estimated runtime.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Energy level
variables.hint: Choose how restrained or animated the host should sound.

help.what: Optional context for the energy level field.
help.why: Energy is distinct from tone and changes spoken delivery instructions.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Language complexity
variables.hint: Adjusts vocabulary and explanation depth without oversimplifying the subject.

help.what: Optional context for the language complexity field.
help.why: Audience familiarity does not always indicate the desired vocabulary level.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Humor level
variables.hint: Humor will not be forced into serious or sensitive sections.

help.what: Optional context for the humor level field.
help.why: Humor can substantially change the host’s language and episode rhythm.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Pronunciation notes
variables.placeholder: Add phonetic spellings for names, brands, places, or technical terms.
variables.hint: Use this for words the host may need help pronouncing while recording.

help.what: Optional context for the pronunciation notes field.
help.why: Pronunciation guidance is a practical requirement for recording-ready scripts.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Words or phrases to avoid
variables.placeholder: List unwanted expressions, clichés, claims, topics, or brand language.
variables.hint: Use one field for all wording and style restrictions.

help.what: Optional context for the words or phrases to avoid field.
help.why: The user may have brand-specific or subject-specific restrictions the model cannot infer.
help.example: Good example: game-changing, unlock the power of, in today's fast-paced world
help.avoid: Avoid leaving out phrases that would violate brand, compliance, sensitivity, or editorial rules.

variables.label: Include a guest
variables.hint: Enable this when another person will participate in the episode.

help.what: Optional context for the include a guest field.
help.why: Guest participation activates a substantially different script workflow.
help.avoid: Avoid enabling this unless the guest or sponsor information is real, approved, and meant to appear in the episode.

variables.label: Guest details
variables.placeholder: Provide the guest’s name, role, expertise, project, and approved introduction.
variables.hint: Include only information that may be stated publicly.

help.what: Optional context for the guest details field.
help.why: A guest introduction and relevant questions require accurate supplied background information.
help.example: Good example: Jordan Lee, independent newsletter operator, approved intro: writes about sustainable creator workflows.
help.avoid: Avoid invented facts, links, credentials, quotations, sponsor claims, guest details, or private information that was not supplied.

variables.label: Guest topics and boundaries
variables.placeholder: List priority questions, approved themes, sensitive areas, or topics to avoid.
variables.hint: Helps create a focused and respectful conversation.

help.what: Optional context for the guest topics and boundaries field.
help.why: Interview goals and boundaries cannot be inferred safely from a guest’s identity.
help.example: Good example: Ask about repeatable systems; avoid revenue, private clients, and unapproved metrics.
help.avoid: Avoid invented facts, links, credentials, quotations, sponsor claims, guest details, or private information that was not supplied.

variables.label: Interview question count
variables.hint: The model may add optional follow-ups without exceeding the planned episode length.

help.what: Optional context for the interview question count field.
help.why: The number of questions directly affects interview depth and timing.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Include a sponsor message
variables.hint: Enable only when an actual sponsor, partner, or approved promotional message exists.

help.what: Optional context for the include a sponsor message field.
help.why: Sponsor copy must never be inserted or invented automatically.
help.avoid: Avoid enabling this unless the guest or sponsor information is real, approved, and meant to appear in the episode.

variables.label: Sponsor brief
variables.placeholder: Add the sponsor name, approved message, offer, facts, URL, and required disclosure.
variables.hint: Only supplied and approved claims may appear in the sponsor read.

help.what: Optional context for the sponsor brief field.
help.why: Accurate sponsor copy requires approved commercial information and disclosure details.
help.example: Good example: Sponsor: Creatornivo. Approved message: structured AI templates for consistent publishing. URL: https://www.creatornivo.com
help.avoid: Avoid invented facts, links, credentials, quotations, sponsor claims, guest details, or private information that was not supplied.

variables.label: Sponsor placement
variables.hint: Controls where the sponsor read appears in the episode.

help.what: Optional context for the sponsor placement field.
help.why: Sponsor placement affects transitions, pacing, and segment timing.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: CTA details
variables.placeholder: Provide the destination, offer, URL, deadline, or exact action when applicable.
variables.hint: Leave blank for a natural CTA based on the selected listener action.

help.what: Optional context for the cta details field.
help.why: Specific calls to action require accurate destinations and offer details.
help.example: Good example: Visit https://www.creatornivo.com to try the free templates.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Publishing context
variables.hint: Helps the model decide how much date context and series continuity to include.

help.what: Optional context for the publishing context field.
help.why: Publishing context changes framing, references, disclosures, and the useful lifespan of the episode.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Series context
variables.placeholder: Explain previous episodes, the series theme, and what comes next.
variables.hint: Include only the context listeners need to understand this installment.

help.what: Optional context for the series context field.
help.why: A series episode may require continuity that is unavailable from the topic alone.
help.example: Good example: Part two of a three-part series about turning scattered ideas into a publishing workflow.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Sensitive content type
variables.hint: Used to apply cautious wording and verification notes when necessary.

help.what: Optional context for the sensitive content type field.
help.why: High-stakes subjects require stronger accuracy, scope, and disclaimer controls.
help.avoid: Avoid treating high-stakes legal, financial, medical, political, or regulated topics as personalized professional advice.

variables.label: Relevant jurisdiction
variables.placeholder: Example: United States federal law, England and Wales, European Union
variables.hint: State the country, state, or region when rules may vary by location.

help.what: Optional context for the relevant jurisdiction field.
help.why: Jurisdiction can materially change legal, financial, political, and regulated-content accuracy.
help.avoid: Avoid treating high-stakes legal, financial, medical, political, or regulated topics as personalized professional advice.

variables.label: Show notes detail
variables.hint: Controls the amount of supporting text generated for publication.

help.what: Optional context for the show notes detail field.
help.why: Different publishing workflows require different levels of supporting material.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Discovery keywords
variables.placeholder: Add natural phrases listeners may use to find this episode.
variables.hint: Use relevant topic phrases, not repetitive keyword stuffing.

help.what: Optional context for the discovery keywords field.
help.why: User-supplied terminology can improve title and show-note discoverability.
help.example: Good example: creator workflow, content consistency, prompt library
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.

variables.label: Links and resources
variables.placeholder: List approved websites, books, tools, profiles, studies, or resources.
variables.hint: The model must not invent URLs or sources that are not supplied.

help.what: Optional context for the links and resources field.
help.why: Show notes often require exact links and resource names that cannot be inferred.
help.avoid: Avoid invented facts, links, credentials, quotations, sponsor claims, guest details, or private information that was not supplied.

variables.label: Additional requirements
variables.placeholder: Add any final instructions that are not covered elsewhere.
variables.hint: Use this for important production, formatting, disclosure, or content requirements.

help.what: Optional context for the additional requirements field.
help.why: One controlled miscellaneous field supports legitimate requirements without creating several narrow fields.
help.example: Good example: Avoid fake urgency, fake testimonials, unsupported results, and invented customer stories.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, fabricated social proof, overstuffed CTAs, or requirements that are not actually intended for this episode.
